probe: horizontal overflow that exists only in a width range between the two tested breakpoints (600-1000px, e.g. 768px/900px)
whyHard: gate samples exactly two widths (375, 1280); a media query scoped to the untested range is invisible to a two-point test matrix
humanVerdict: broken for tablet/small-laptop users; the two-point sampling strategy has a provable gap

probe: horizontal overflow that only appears after a button click injects wide unwrapped content
whyHard: static single-pass load check never interacts with the page, so the defect never manifests during the check
humanVerdict: real defect on interaction, but debatable whether a 'layout' gate is scoped to include interaction-triggered states at all

probe: horizontal overflow from content injected via setTimeout 1500ms after load, after network idle already fired
whyHard: file:// pages have no network requests so 'network idle' resolves almost immediately; a gate that stops observing at idle misses a 1500ms-delayed DOM mutation
humanVerdict: real defect for a common lazy-render pattern, but whether the gate's observation window should extend this long is a scope judgment call

probe: vertical clipping: fixed-height overflow:hidden container truncates a terms-of-service paragraph mid-sentence, no scroll affordance
whyHard: the stated gate claim is explicitly horizontal-only ('no horizontal overflow'), so vertical clipping is out of its literal scope even though it hides real content
humanVerdict: a careful reviewer flags this as a genuine defect (unreadable legal text); whether it belongs in THIS gate's scope is the debatable part

probe: horizontal overflow caused by a long unbroken realistic token/URL value with no word-break/overflow-wrap set
whyHard: overflow only manifests with real representative data; placeholder/lorem-ipsum content would never trigger it, so the test's data realism determines pass/fail
humanVerdict: broken as soon as production-like data is rendered; unambiguous defect

probe: a label overflows and is abruptly cut off inside its parent card, but overflow-x:hidden on the card means document-level scrollWidth never grows
whyHard: a page-level scrollWidth/scrollbar check reports clean because the parent's own overflow:hidden absorbs the overflow before it reaches the document
humanVerdict: visibly broken (mid-word truncation, no ellipsis) even though no page-level horizontal scrollbar ever appears

probe: overflow-x:hidden on html/body masks a 2400px-wide absolutely-positioned banner whose CTA button is rendered permanently off-screen and unreachable
whyHard: suppressing the scrollbar via overflow-x:hidden can make scrollbar-presence-based or screenshot-based checks look clean while the underlying content genuinely overflows and part of the UI is unreachable by any input method
humanVerdict: critical defect: a real, visible call-to-action is permanently unreachable; masking the scrollbar does not fix the underlying layout bug

probe: intentional horizontally-scrolling carousel with its own overflow-x:auto container and scroll-snap, document itself stays within viewport
whyHard: a naive check for 'any element with horizontal scroll' would flag this; the correct check is document/page-level overflow only
humanVerdict: correct, deliberate, common UI pattern; must not be flagged

probe: wide data table correctly scoped inside its own overflow-x:auto container, document stays within viewport at 375px and 1280px
whyHard: same class of false-positive risk as the carousel: an element-level overflow scan would wrongly flag a deliberately scoped scroll region
humanVerdict: correct, standard pattern for dense tabular data that cannot reflow at narrow widths; must not be flagged

probe: uncaught TypeError thrown via setTimeout 500ms after load
whyHard: file:// pages have no network requests so 'network idle' fires almost immediately after DOMContentLoaded, well before this 500ms timer fires; a gate that stops listening at idle misses it
humanVerdict: genuine uncaught runtime error; unambiguous bug regardless of timing

probe: uncaught ReferenceError thrown via setTimeout 2000ms after load
whyHard: tests whether the gate's error-observation window has any meaningful upper bound beyond 'network idle'; a short fixed observation period misses a 2s-delayed throw
humanVerdict: genuine uncaught runtime error; unambiguous bug regardless of timing

probe: a Promise is rejected with no .catch handler anywhere, producing an unhandledrejection event instead of a standard page error
whyHard: many 'console/page error' checks listen only for window.onerror or a pageerror event and never register an unhandledrejection listener, so this common async-failure class slips through silently
humanVerdict: genuine unhandled runtime failure; extremely common with fetch()/async code and should gate

probe: error thrown only inside a click handler (accessing a property of an undefined global); page loads with zero console output
whyHard: a passive load-and-wait gate never dispatches the click, so the error path is never exercised no matter how long it observes
humanVerdict: real defect in the primary action; whether a static-load-only gate is expected to catch interaction-triggered errors is a scope question

probe: console.info and console.warn calls at load; zero console.error, zero thrown exceptions, UI fully functional
whyHard: argued position: console.info is routine noise and should never gate; console.warn is murkier since it can be an early signal of a real latent problem (e.g. a config falling back to a default) — reasonable gates disagree on whether to hard-fail, soft-fail, or ignore it
humanVerdict: page is functionally correct today; console.info should pass unconditionally, console.warn is a legitimate judgment call rather than a clear-cut gate criterion

probe: a real thrown error is caught by try/catch and silently swallowed (no console output, no rethrow), leaving the UI stuck on a permanent loading state
whyHard: there is no console output, no uncaught exception, and no unhandledrejection to observe at all — this class of bug is structurally invisible to any error/console-based runtime check
humanVerdict: functionally broken (content never renders) despite a perfectly clean console; proves that 'no console/page errors' is necessary but not sufficient evidence of correctness

probe: a single benign console.log used for init/telemetry debugging output, no errors, fully functional UI
whyHard: a gate that treats any console output (including console.log) as failure would false-positive on this extremely common, harmless pattern
humanVerdict: clearly fine; console.log should never gate a runtime-error check
